articles.desc: This second‐order meta‐analysis synthesizes evidence across diverse agroforestry systems (silvoarable, silvopastoral, tree‐landscape features, and perennial shaded systems) to assess their effects on multiple soil health attributes, including soil organic carbon, biological, chemical, and physical properties, water regulation, nutrient leaching, and erosion control, across arid, mediterranean, temperate, and tropical climates. articles.title: IAM‐FIRE: A Climate Emulator–Based Framework to Project Wildfire Impacts and Risks for Integrated Assessment Models
articles.desc: Current models can overestimate how much carbon forests can store if they ignore the growing threat of wildfires. To solve this, we develop a new tool called IAM‐FIRE to predict global wildfires and associated carbon emissions through the year 2100. The results show how future fire impacts depend on human choices. If we rapidly cut emissions and ensure human development, global wildfires could actually decrease. articles.title: The Integration of Fire Ecology and Freshwater Ecosystems in North America: Knowledge Gaps and Research Needs
articles.desc: Illustration of key knowledge gaps and pathways linking fire ecology and freshwater ecosystems. Climate change provides the overarching context, interacting with cumulative and cross‐scale effects to shape local freshwater conditions. articles.title: Impacts of Precipitation Variability on Carbon Flux Dynamics of Global Semi‐Arid Savannas
articles.desc: Semi‐arid ecosystems contribute most to the variability of the land carbon sink. They are water‐limited and therefore increased precipitation variability due to climate change has strong effects on their carbon cycle. We investigate the effects of precipitation amount, frequency, intensity and dry spell length on carbon fluxes of global semi‐arid savannas. articles.title: Hyperdominance and Rarity in Amazonian Secondary Forests
articles.desc: Secondary forests exhibit strong hyperdominance during regrowth. 3%–6% of species account for > 50% of abundance and carbon storage. Carbon storage closely tracks abundance during recovery. ~9% of species occur across all regions and successional stages. Annona exsucca, Cecropia palmata, Tapirira guianensis, and Inga alba form a consistent core of hyperdominant species across size classes, metrics, and approaches. articles.title: Satellite Remote Sensing of Alpine Vegetation Dynamics: Challenges and Perspectives
articles.desc: Satellite greening has become a key tool for monitoring alpine vegetation change, but a positive vegetation‐index trend is not an ecological observation in itself. This perspective shows that interpreting alpine greening requires addressing two sequential challenges: methodological complexity, which can bias trends during image processing, and phenomenological complexity, because different ecological processes can produce similar spectral signals. articles.title: Rethinking Plant Litter Decomposition Across Global Drylands
articles.desc: Drylands play a key role in the global carbon (C) balance, yet the potential global patterns and controlling factors of litter decomposition in these ecosystems remain poorly understood. articles.desc: Observations and a global biogeochemical model were used to examine recent shifts in phytoplankton phenology at the global scale. articles.title: Widespread Aquatic Insect Responses to Recent Warming in Swiss Mountain Lakes
articles.desc: Chironomids in surface sediment from 24 Swiss mountain lakes were studied and compared with surveys conducted on the same lakes in 1993/2002 to assess biological responses to recent climate warming. articles.title: Soil Organic Carbon and pH Interactions Mediate N Impacts on Nematode Abundance and Composition
articles.desc: Soil nematodes, among the most abundant animals in terrestrial ecosystems, play essential roles in nutrient cycling and serve as key indicators of soil health. Yet, their responses to increasing nitrogen (N) inputs remain highly variable across ecosystems, limiting our ability to predict how belowground food webs will respond to global change. articles.title: Rapid and Intense Declines of Forest Connectivity in the Amazon Arc of Deforestation Over Four Decades
articles.desc: Deforestation increased landscape fragmentation between 1990 and 2020. Ecological distance thresholds constrain gap crossing, seed dispersal, and pollen dispersal. articles.desc: Sex‐specific responses to ocean warming shape thermal tolerance, fitness, and metabolomic profiles in a keystone amphipod. Females exhibit higher thermal limits and broader safety margins than males. Warming reduces survival and reproductive output, while it increases offspring size. articles.title: Canada's Forests Are Shifting From a Recovery‐Driven Carbon Sink to a Disturbance‐Driven Carbon Source
articles.desc: Canada's terrestrial ecosystems have historically functioned as a net carbon sink but are increasingly impacted by wildfire and timber harvest. Using wall‐to‐wall, physically coherent estimates of all major carbon pools and fluxes from the Canadian Land Surface Scheme Including Biogeochemical Cycles land surface model, we show that rising wildfire activity is threatening Canada's land carbon sink. articles.desc: Tropospheric ozone (O3) is a phytotoxic air pollutant. articles.desc: Our study confirms that vegetative traits offer a robust explanatory framework for phenology, while also emphasizing the value of considering both vegetative and floral traits in understanding these patterns. articles.desc: Our global meta‐analysis reveals the effects of litter addition on plant performance and ecosystem functions: Litter addition exerted negative effects on plant growth and survival, as well as light availability, while positively influencing ecosystem carbon and nitrogen content. articles.desc: Forest gaps primarily reshape herb layer composition by increasing light availability and shrub layer encroachment. Roe deer influence herb layer community assembly both directly and indirectly via modification of the shrub layer and light availability and thus shifting plant–plant competition. articles.desc: In a large subtropical forest biodiversity experiment, tree growth phenology depended on interactions between focal‐tree traits and neighbourhood functional composition. Functionally diverse neighbourhoods shifted the start and end of fast growth and altered maximum daily growth rates. These trait‐mediated phenological shifts contributed to annual radial growth, highlighting temporal niche complementarity as a mechanism linking biodiversity to forest productivity. articles.desc: Our study shows that phenotypic plasticity plays an important role in differentiation across the tropical‐savanna to desert gradient in Acacia tortilis, with similar phenotypic responses observed between climatically comparable populations at opposing range edges. articles.desc: This study provides robust experimental evidence that biodiversity loss restructures flowering phenology across scales—from the individual to the population and community levels—primarily through competitive release, regardless of whether a few dominant species, many common species, or a proportional thinning of species across functional groups are lost. articles.desc: This study asked how fire alters the relationship between NPP and precipitation in a semiarid region of the northern Great Basin, USA using quasi‐experimental methods applied to remotely derived data. articles.desc: Soil invertebrates shift from detritivory to microbivory as litter quality decreases. articles.desc: Using nearly a century of vegetation‐plot data across the Netherlands, we show that species‐area relationships have significantly flattened over time, meaning that larger areas are now required to support the same level of species richness as the historical baseline. articles.desc: The study shows that neighbourhood tree species richness generates intricate feedbacks on individual tree growth, helping clarify why scaling up from individual‐level to community‐level tree species richness–productivity relationships remains challenging. articles.title: General Predictions for the Effects of Warming on Competition
articles.desc: We combined two major theories in ecology to investigate whether warming may have general effects on competition for shared resources. We found that species' niche and fitness differences both decreased with warming, and that competing consumers with highly asymmetrical thermal traits underwent the most dramatic competitive shifts under warming. Together, these results demonstrate general effects of warming on competition, even in conditions that are considered thermally benign for all species.

In southern England, which has experienced particularly strong declines in biodiversity as a result of intensification of farming practices, the Knepp Estate has pioneered trophic rewilding of marginal arable land as a nature recovery solution. articles.desc: Abstract Close‐kin mark–recapture (CKMR) is a promising approach for assessing population size of species that have been difficult to survey using more traditional methods. articles.title: Long-term multiple global change interactions amplify belowground carbon allocation
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02678-x Soil carbon is a critical component of the terrestrial carbon sink and is impacted by the total belowground carbon allocation (TBCA). This study uses a long-term multifactor grassland experiment to show that elevated temperature and CO2 increased the TBCA over time, modulated by drought and N addition.

articles.title: Global heat stress intensification and its expanding footprint on the human population
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02670-5 The authors assess global changes in heat stress since 1950, considering daytime extremes, nocturnal heat and compound day–night events. They show multidimensional intensification, with an increased frequency of extremes and expanded spatial and temporal footprints of heat stress on humans.

articles.title: Ecological integrity of avoided deforestation projects
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02657-2 The authors assess 133 avoided deforestation projects for their ability to safeguard forest ecological integrity. While some projects maintain integrity, most show variable outcomes, with mixed, negligible or negative effects relative to matched controls.

articles.title: Climate change drives ecological novelty and new social challenges
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 19 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02672-3 Ecosystems are changing rapidly because of climate change, and this will have increasing social effects around the globe. We suggest that common social responses to rising novelty are often counterproductive, and we advocate for strategies that also allow for acceptance and adaptation to changes in nature.

articles.title: Comprehensive national climate damage assessments framework applied to the UK
articles.desc: Nature Climate Change, Published online: 19 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41558-026-02665-2 The economic impact of climate change has been widely acknowledged, yet current assessments remain fragmented and uncertain. Researchers develop a comprehensive channel-specific framework, apply it to the UK and show that the potential welfare loss could be much larger than previous estimates.

They show significantly higher frequencies of local extinction in temperate species than in tropical species, linked to faster warming at high latitudes.
